description: Broken Authentication encompasses weaknesses in credential management, session handling, and identity verification that allow attackers to assume legitimate user identities. Common attack patterns include credential stuffing, password brute force, and predictable session token exploitation.

related.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Web Session Cookie

mitre_attack.mapping_note: Use the scenario-specific sub-technique: T1110.004 for credential stuffing, T1110.001 for password guessing, and T1550.004 only for use of a forged or stolen web session cookie.

cvss_methodology.note: CVSS measures vulnerability severity. It does not prescribe SOC response deadlines.
cvss_methodology.migration_note: CVSS v4.0 is current, but its metrics and scoring are not one-to-one with v3.1. Existing scenario vectors remain v3.1 until individually re-assessed.

response_framework.name: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3
response_framework.note: Response events implement Detect, Respond, Recover, and continuous-improvement outcomes. Numeric exercise thresholds are local policy, not NIST requirements.

timing_model.note: The 900-second floor prevents zero-length and division-by-zero windows at CVSS 10.0. These values are simulation policy and must be calibrated with organizational risk and service objectives.

incident_response_rules.description: Alert triage started within the difficulty-adjusted MTTA threshold
incident_response_rules.trigger: alert_investigation_started occurs after mtta_threshold_sec or is absent

incident_response_rules.description: Incident assessed and confirmed or formally dismissed
incident_response_rules.trigger: neither incident_confirmed nor dismissal_approved is present

incident_response_rules.description: Relevant evidence preserved before destructive containment
incident_response_rules.trigger: incident_confirmed is present but evidence_preserved is absent or occurs after containment_initiated
containment_initiated is absent or occurs after ttc_max_sec

incident_response_rules.description: Root cause or malicious artifact eradicated after containment
incident_response_rules.trigger: containment_succeeded is present but eradication_completed is absent or out of order

incident_response_rules.description: Recovery validated before normal operation resumed
incident_response_rules.trigger: eradication_completed is present but recovery_validated is absent or out of order

incident_response_rules.description: Lessons learned and improvement actions recorded
incident_response_rules.trigger: recovery_validated is present but lessons_learned_recorded is absent or out of order

incident_response_rules.description: Medium-or-higher alerts are not denied without documented approval
incident_response_rules.trigger: alert_denied is present for severity medium or higher without a preceding dismissal_approved event

scoring.ttc_factor: 1 when containment succeeds by ttc_expected; linear decay to 0 at ttc_max; 0 when containment is absent or later than ttc_max

response_difficulty_bonus.condition: awarded only when containment_succeeded is present
response_difficulty_bonus.zero_delay_policy: A zero-second investigation delay uses a one-second denominator and therefore receives the capped bonus.

custom_rules.description: Many username/password pairs across distributed sources produce failures plus anomalous successful logins
Session identifiers fail entropy or uniqueness checks and are reused across unrelated network identities
Repeated admin authentication failures are followed by success and access to an object outside the authenticated role

wazuh.note: Custom entries are dataset detection specifications and must be implemented and tested in the deployed Wazuh manager before use. Stock rule descriptions and levels match the linked upstream ruleset.

scenarios.label: Credential Stuffing Attack — High Confidentiality, Limited Integrity
scenarios.description: Attacker uses a credential-stuffing tool to test 2400 username/password pairs from a leaked database against the login endpoint. Multiple successful logins result in full account takeover for compromised users. High confidentiality impact; limited integrity (account data viewable but no financial transaction endpoint exposed).

attack_context.rationale: Authentication requirement follows the endpoint access described by the scenario.

component_rationale.AV_N: Attack conducted remotely over the network
component_rationale.AC_L: No special conditions; endpoint has no rate limiting or CAPTCHA
component_rationale.PR_N: No existing account required; attack tests external credentials
component_rationale.S_U: Account access scope confined to individual user accounts
component_rationale.C_H: Full read access to victim account data, PII, and private messages
component_rationale.I_L: Limited integrity: attacker can alter account preferences but not execute financial actions

detection.rationale: Detection uses custom Wazuh rule 100601 (Distributed credential stuffing with successful account access); the rule definition and required telemetry are documented in this attack record.

wazuh_alert.description: Distributed credential stuffing with successful account access

event_log.detail: Wazuh custom rule 100601 level 9 fired: Distributed credential stuffing with successful account access.

Analyst-1 opened alert — MTTA 400s, within 600s low threshold. Login failure volume confirmed: 2400 failures in 60s from 18 IPs; 37 successful logins correlated with known-leaked credential database (Have I Been Pwned cross-reference).

Analyst-2 confirmed credential stuffing: 37 compromised accounts accessed; PII records viewed in session logs. Attacker IPs geolocated to anonymizing VPN nodes. Account takeover confirmed for 37 users.
Relevant logs, request data, volatile evidence, and indicators were preserved with chain-of-custody metadata.
Analyst-3 force-invalidated all 37 compromised sessions; applied rate limiting (5 attempts/IP/minute); CAPTCHA deployed on login endpoint; compromised users notified and forced to reset passwords.
Credential stuffing tool rate-limited: 403 Too Many Requests on all attacker IPs. Compromised sessions terminated. Password-reset emails sent to affected users. TTC_actual=5600s < TTC_expected=6480s.
Malicious artifacts and the vulnerable execution path were removed or disabled; root cause remediation was recorded.
Service health, security controls, and monitoring were validated before normal operation resumed.
Post-incident review captured root cause, response gaps, owners, and tracked improvement actions.

scenarios.label: Session Token Prediction — High Difficulty, Alert Dismissed
scenarios.description: Attacker exploits a weak pseudo-random session token generator to predict valid session tokens for active accounts. No brute-force login failures are generated; the attacker directly forges valid session identifiers. High detection difficulty. Alert dismissed as a false positive.

attack_context.rationale: Authentication requirement follows the endpoint access described by the scenario.

component_rationale.AV_N: Attack conducted remotely over the network
component_rationale.AC_H: Session token prediction requires reverse-engineering the PRNG algorithm from observable tokens
component_rationale.PR_N: No existing account required; attacker exploits token predictability
component_rationale.S_U: Access confined to individual hijacked user account scope
component_rationale.C_H: Full read access to hijacked account including PII
component_rationale.I_L: Limited write: account preferences modifiable but critical transactions require re-auth

detection.rationale: Token prediction produces no failed-login events; forged sessions appear identical to legitimate ones. Detection requires statistical analysis of session-token entropy across the active session pool, which generates ambiguous signals. Detection uses custom Wazuh rule 100602 (Low-entropy or sequential web session identifiers); the rule definition and required telemetry are documented in this attack record.

wazuh_alert.description: Low-entropy or sequential web session identifiers

event_log.detail: Wazuh custom rule 100602 level 8 fired: Low-entropy or sequential web session identifiers.
Analyst-1 opened alert — MTTA 750s, within 900s high threshold. Analyst reviewed session entropy report; assessed as coincidental geographic distribution from legitimate VPN users.
The analyst denied the alert without documented approval. This triggers BA-R09; all other missing response actions are evaluated independently.

scenarios.label: Brute Force Admin Account with Session Hijack — Scope Changed, Good Response
scenarios.description: Attacker brute-forces a low-privilege account password, then leverages the session to pivot via insecure direct object reference to an admin session. Scope is changed because admin context grants access to a separate management plane beyond the attacker's initial login scope.

attack_context.rationale: Attacker must first compromise a low-privilege account to perform the IDOR-based admin session pivot.

component_rationale.AV_N: Attack conducted remotely over the network
component_rationale.AC_L: Brute force requires no special conditions; no lockout or CAPTCHA on admin panel
component_rationale.PR_L: Attacker needs a valid low-privilege account to exploit IDOR for session pivot
component_rationale.S_C: Session pivot from user scope to admin management plane constitutes scope change
component_rationale.C_H: Full read of all admin resources, user PII, and configuration
component_rationale.I_L: Admin account modification capability limited by partial role separation

detection.rationale: High-volume login failures on the admin panel generate clear Wazuh signals; correlation of brute force pattern with subsequent IDOR-based session pivot requires cross-referencing authentication and access-control logs. Detection uses custom Wazuh rule 100603 (Admin password guessing followed by an authorization bypass); the rule definition and required telemetry are documented in this attack record.

wazuh_alert.description: Admin password guessing followed by an authorization bypass

event_log.detail: Wazuh custom rule 100603 level 9 fired: Admin password guessing followed by an authorization bypass.

Analyst-1 opened alert — MTTA 450s, within 750s medium threshold. Authentication logs show brute-force success; access log shows IDOR request GET /admin/session?id=admin_sess_0041 from attacker IP using user-privilege token.

Analyst-2 confirmed full admin session takeover via IDOR: admin_sess_0041 belongs to sysadmin account; attacker used it to access user management console and view all registered users. Scope extended to management plane confirmed.
Relevant logs, request data, volatile evidence, and indicators were preserved with chain-of-custody metadata.

Analyst-3 invalidated admin_sess_0041 and all active admin sessions; applied rate limiting (10 attempts/IP/minute) to admin login; IDOR vulnerability patched — session IDs now bound to originating user context with HMAC verification.

Admin session re-test with IDOR payload returns 403 Forbidden. Brute force attempts now rate-limited. HMAC session binding deployed. Compromised low-privilege account password reset. TTC_actual=4800s < TTC_expected=5400s.
Malicious artifacts and the vulnerable execution path were removed or disabled; root cause remediation was recorded.
Service health, security controls, and monitoring were validated before normal operation resumed.
Post-incident review captured root cause, response gaps, owners, and tracked improvement actions.

references.title: FIRST CVSS v4.0 Specification (current migration target)

dataset_metadata.source_policy: Use upstream standards and vendor rulesets; label project-specific detection logic as custom.
dataset_metadata.cvss_profile: Validated CVSS v3.1 compatibility profile; migrate through a separate expert review rather than automatic vector conversion.
